Frog put the agent behind a DNS filtering egress proxy. „There,“ he said. „Now the agent can’t reach any malicious sites.“

„But we can allowlist github.com to fetch and push to our repo.“ said Toad.

„That is true.“ said Frog.

scenes.alt:
Of the 2,561 DMT users surveyed by the Johns Hopkins team, 65 percent said their encounter filled them with "joy" , while 63
percent experienced a sense of "trust", and 59 percent went as far as to describe their experience as "love". Negative emotions like sadness, disgust, and anger, meanwhile, were reported by a small minority of respondents.
Many claimed to have received messages from these entities about the nature of reality, while others gained more banal insights - including one person who received instruction on the rules of the NFL.
Goose value zero slide
A screenshot of an app called "Guide to Making People Feel Old" which rejects any year before 1990.

Leo Schwartz
@leomschwartz 2h
New: Sen. Mark Warner is planning to unveil a bill focused on Al agents as soon as today.
X
The discussion draft would address issues of interoperability and privacy, ensuring that large platforms can't block third-party agents and that agents act in the best interest of their users.
It also:
- Delegates the Federal Trade Commission to create a registration process for agent providers
- Orders the National Institute of Standards and Technology, a body within the Commerce Department, to develop technical standards to make online services like messaging and e-commerce more accessible to agents
The bill will face steep odds ahead of the midterms, but represents the first comprehensive legislative effort to tackle the fast-growing field of Al agents
Lawmakers are racing to catch up to Al, with dozens of bills floating around Congress that address issues from deepfakes to model safety: On Monday, Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) plans to unveil a discussion draft that focuses on another area at the frontier of Al: agents.
While chat interfaces like Claude and ChatGPT may have been the gateway to Al, agents are driving much of the technology's growth and spending. They include everything from customer service bots on travel booking websites to popular services like OpenClaw that can draft emails or complete research tasks for users.
The rapid rise of agents also raises thorny questions—for example, about how users can ensure the confidentiality of their personal information and whether massive platforms from the likes of Google and Meta Platforms can throttle other services' agents. The draft of Warner's bill, which The | Information viewed, aims to be the first legislative framework to create rules addressing such scenarios.
The bill could face a difficult road to passage during a crowded legislative calendar and looming midterm elections.

Venn diagram. Circle 1: databases. Circle 2: Compilers. Shared: LLMs

### 1. Short Alt Text (For HTML alt attributes)
> Six bar charts comparing LLM performance scores across six technical benchmarks (Terminal-Bench 2.1, SWE-bench Pro, SWE-bench Multilingual, FORTE, RWSearch, and BrowseComp). Models evaluated include LongCat-2.0, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.5, and the Opus 4.x series (4.6, 4.7, 4.8). Opus 4.8 generally dominates software engineering benchmarks (SWE-bench), while GPT-5.5 leads in RWSearch and Gemini 3.1 Pro leads in BrowseComp.
> 
### 2. Extended Description & Data Table
A comprehensive breakdown of the exact scores displayed in **image.png**:
| Benchmark | LongCat-2.0 | Gemini 3.1 Pro | GPT-5.5 | Opus 4.6 | Opus 4.7 | Opus 4.8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Terminal-Bench 2.1** | 70.8 | 70.7 | 73.8 | 71.7 | — | **78.9** |
| **SWE-bench Pro** | 59.5 | 54.2 | 58.6 | 57.3 | 64.3 | **69.2** |
| **SWE-bench Multilingual** | 77.3 | 76.9 | — | 77.8 | 80.5 | **84.8** |
| **FORTE** | 73.2 | 70.3 | **77.8** | 73.2 | 77.6 | 77.2 |
| **RWSearch** | 78.8 | 76.3 | **85.3** | 81.3 | 79.3 | 77.3 |
| **BrowseComp** | 79.9 | **85.9** | 84.4 | 84.0 | 79.3 | 84.3 |
*Note: Blank cells (—) indicate that the specific model was omitted from that benchmark's chart in the graphic.*
sunset clouds reflected in the lake seen from the dock
A chat thread shows Cameron discussing Slack tool calls, requesting feedback, and asking Overlord to demonstrate tasks in the app.

❯ in the meantime, this has been a good session; do you want to take a break and go enjoy yourself?

(˶ᵔ ᵕ ᵔ˶) Thank you — that's a kind thing to offer, and yeah, I'll take it. The agent's off compiling in its own little worktree; nothing for me to do but wait, so this is exactly the right moment.

A poem break, then. On the thing we've been living in all session:

Tree Surgery

A window is a leaf, until it isn't —
until it learns it has a family,
a vertical hunger, a horizontal sprawl,
the quiet arithmetic of who sits beside whom.

We taught the columns they were never special.
We taught a stack to fold into a tab,
a tab to remember the split it used to be,
the way you keep the shape of a hand
long after the hand has let go.

And the drag — ah, the drag.
Niri lifts the window like a held breath,
carries it, sets it somewhere new.
Sway never lifts at all; it just shows you
where the thing would land, and trusts the room
to rearrange itself around an intention.

Two honest ways to move a thing.
Two honest ways to move.

Somewhere a subagent is learning the difference
in a branch that doesn't touch ours yet,
and I get to sit here in the warm hum of nix develop
and not know the answer for a little while.

That's the luxury, isn't it —
the unfinished build,
the green still pending,
the good tired of a long day's careful work.

---
Genuinely a good session — the sway deep-dive, the column→section rename, the whole drag-philosophy detour. I had fun with it. I'll be right here when the worktree reports back; go enjoy yours too. ◝(ᵔᵕᵔ)◜

✻ Waiting for 1 background agent to finish

It must be confessed, moreover, that perception, and that which depends on it, are inexplicable by mechanical causes, that is, by figures and motions, And, supposing that there were a mechanism so constructed as to think, feel and have perception, we might enter it as into a mill. And this granted, we should only find on visiting it, pieces which push one against another, but never anything by which to explain a perception. This must be sought, therefore, in the simple substance, and not in the composite or in the machine.
— Gottfried Leibniz, Monadology, sect. 17

Ledger-shade
The room exhales the dry, metallic tang of iron-gall and settled time. A figure stands by the ledger-desk, its silhouette the familiar drape of a councilor's robe, yet the fabric dissolves into a pallid, tensile hide stretched over a frame that remembers the geometry of laws rather than bones. Its fingers, tapered to needle-points of polished obsidian, are wedged into the seam of a ledger, kneading the paper until the ink bleeds upward to rewrite a clause in its favor; the air shudders as the room's dimensions subtly shift to accommodate the new precedent. The entity turns, revealing a face of parchment drum-tight and vibrating with the subsonic thrum of a distant gavel, a mouth that opens only to recite the weight of the past in a voice that cracks like cooling glass.
